coredata.dc:title: Strong composition-dependence on glass-forming ability in Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si pseudo-ternary alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, H.
Wang, J.Q.
Li, Y.
coredata.dc:description: The glass formation in Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si pseudo-ternary alloys was studied. For suction casting, by carefully adjusting the alloy composition and studying the microstructure changes, the best glass-forming alloy with a 2mm diameter is pinpointed in a narrow composition region of 57.5–58.5at.% Ni, 36.5–38.5at.% (Ti+Zr) and 5–5.5at.% Si. The main competing crystalline phases, identified by XRD and SEM, were Ni10(Zr,Ti)7, Ni(Ti,Zr) and an unidentified Si-containing phase. Our results indicate a clear need for monitoring the microstructure change in the cross section of the ingots to locate the best glass-forming alloys.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metallic glasses
Ni-based alloy
Casting
Thermal analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Composition range for bulk glass formation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Compositional range in which Ni–(Ti, Zr)–Si alloys were studied.
Melting curves of master alloys 1–7 at a heating rate of 20K/min. Noted that these alloys correspond to the same one eutectic reaction.
(a–f) Optical micrographs of the cross section of the as-cast rods with 2mm diameter for alloys 2–7, illustrating microstructure changes in these samples.
Optical micrographs of the cross sections of the as-cast rods with 2mm diameter for: (a) alloy 2 and (b) alloy 3 at high magnification.
XRD patterns taken from the cross sections of the as-cast rods with 2mm diameter for alloys 2–7.
(a–f) Optical micrographs of the cross sections of the as-cast rods with 2mm diameter for alloys 1, 11–13, 19 and 20, illustrating microstructure changes in these samples.
Optical micrographs of the cross section of the as-cast rods with 2mm diameter for: (a) alloy 1 and (b) alloy 13 at high magnification.
XRD patterns taken from the cross sections of the as-cast rods with 2mm diameter for alloys 1, 11–13, 19, and 20.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) DSC curves at a heating rate of 20K/min of the as-cast rod with 2mm diameter and as-spun ribbon of alloy 11.
Compositional range for glass formation with the critical size of 2mm in Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si system, reflecting a strong GFA dependency on composition.
Alloy numbers with corresponding composition of Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si alloys
Thermal properties and structure characteristic of glassy alloys in Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si system

article.ce:title: Strong composition-dependence on glass-forming ability in Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si pseudo-ternary alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenyang National Laboratory for Materials Science, Institute of Metal Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 72 Wenhua Road, Shenyang 110016, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Engineering Faculty, National University of Singapore, Engineering Drive 1, Singapore 117576, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The glass formation in Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si pseudo-ternary alloys was studied. For suction casting, by carefully adjusting the alloy composition and studying the microstructure changes, the best glass-forming alloy with a 2mm diameter is pinpointed in a narrow composition region of 57.5–58.5at.% Ni, 36.5–38.5at.% (Ti+Zr) and 5–5.5at.% Si. The main competing crystalline phases, identified by XRD and SEM, were Ni10(Zr,Ti)7, Ni(Ti,Zr) and an unidentified Si-containing phase. Our results indicate a clear need for monitoring the microstructure change in the cross section of the ingots to locate the best glass-forming alloys.

ce:section.ce:para: Many bulk metallic glasses (BMGs) have been developed over the last decades as engineering materials because of their unique and superior mechanical properties [1,2]. These BMGs have been fabricated by conventional copper mould casting in many alloys with critical sizes larger than 10mm, e.g. Zr- [3], Pd- [4], Fe- [5], Cu- [6] and Mg- [7] based alloys. However, the success in producing bulk glasses in Ni-based alloys is rather limited [8–13] despite the fact that the Ni-based amorphous alloys have drawn increasing attention due to their excellent mechanical properties and good corrosion resistance. Amorphous rods of 2, 3 and 5mm diameter have been developed in the Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si [9], Ni–(Ti,Zr)–(Si,Sn) [10] and Ni–(Nb,Ti,Zr)–(Si,Sn) [11] systems, respectively. As documented before [1,2,14,15], the increase of the number of components involved in the alloys will generally improve the GFA of alloys. Therefore, among these Ni-based alloy systems, Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si system is an important one as it can be regarded as the base system for studying their glass-forming ability (GFA). Finding the best glass former in the Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si system will provide a real starting point for the subsequent study of GFA in the higher order multicomponent systems such as in the Ni–(Ti,Zr,Nb)–(Si,Sn).

It is well known that the alloys with strong GFA are often associated with deep eutectic and the alloy composition has a strong influence on the GFA for a certain alloy system. Thus, we expect that the improvement in GFA could be realized by adjusting composition in the Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si system. In this paper, by carefully studying the microstructure evolution upon making changes in the alloy composition, we systematically studied the influence of composition on the GFA in the Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si system.

Ni (99.99at.%), Zr (99.9at.%), Ti (99.9at.%) and Si (99.999at.%) were used as raw materials for arc melting under a Ti-gettered argon atmosphere on a water-cooled copper plate. The alloys were melted at least six times to ensure composition uniformity. Rapidly solidified ribbons with a cross section of 0.03mm×4mm were prepared by a singer roller melt-spinning technique in an argon atmosphere. Rod-shaped samples were cast by the suction of the molten alloy into the cavity of a copper mold with a diameter of 2mm and length of 30mm. The glass transition, crystallization and melting behavior of samples were analyzed by a Neztsch DSC 404C under flowing purified argon at a heating rate of 20K/min. The amorphicity of the cast rods was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) in a Rigaku D/max 2400 diffractometer (Tokyo, Japan) with monochromated Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.1542nm). The as-cast rods were etched by a solution of nitric acid, hydrofluoric acid and vitriol (10:2:1), and then were examined by an optical microscope and a Cambridge S360 scanning electron microscope (SEM) with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis.
Fig. 1 shows the compositional range of the Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si alloys studied. The alloy Ni57Ti18Zr20Si5 (alloy 1) reported by Yi et al. [9] was selected for comparison.

After fixing the ratio of Ti to Zr at 18:20 and regarding Ti and Zr as similar components, a pseudo-ternary Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si diagram was attained. All alloys studied in this work lie in this pseudo-ternary system. The designation of the alloys with the corresponding compositions is given in Table 1 .

The melting curves of alloys 1–7 at a heating rate of 20K/min are shown in Fig. 2 . The T m and T L marked by arrows correspond to the solidus and liquidus temperature, respectively. It is apparent that the T m stays at about 1257K for these alloys and each curve exhibits multiple melting peaks, indicating that they correspond to the same one eutectic reaction but none of them is at eutectic composition. Thus, the alloys studied in the Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si system can be indeed treated as belonging to a pseudo-ternary eutectic system.

In this section, we will demonstrate how the best glass formers are pinpointed with an accuracy of ±0.5at.% through metallographic observation. Our chemical analysis result (not shown) confirms that the compositional accuracy of alloys is within error allowance.

Fig. 3 shows the optical micrographs of 2mm diameter as-cast rods of alloys 2–7, which illustrate distinct different morphologies. Optical micrographs of alloys 2 and 3 in the high magnification are shown in Fig. 4 . Alloys 4 (Ni59Ti18Zr20Si3) and 7 (Ni59Ti16.9Zr19.1Si5) show eutectic cell-like microstructures (Fig. 3). Alloys 2 (Ni57Ti18.8Zr21.2Si3) (Fig. 4a) and 6 (Ni55Ti18.2Zr21.2Si5) have apparent dendrite structures, while alloys 3 (Ni57Ti16.9Zr19.1Si7) (Fig. 4b) and 5 (Ni55Ti18Zr20Si7) have a small amount of fine dendrites randomly dispersed in the crystalline matrix. The EDX results show that these fine dendrites are Si-containing. In Fig. 5 , the XRD patterns exhibit sharp diffraction peaks corresponding mainly to orthorhombic Ni10(Zr,Ti)7 and cubic Ni(Ti,Zr) phases. DSC curves (not shown) confirm that no amorphous phase can be formed in the as-cast 2mm rods of the above six alloys.

Fig. 6 displays the optical micrographs of 2mm diameter as-cast rods of alloys 1–13, 19 and 20, which illustrate the microstructure changes of these alloys. Alloys 1 (Ni57Ti18Zr20Si5), 12 (Ni58Ti18.1Zr19.4Si4.5), 13 (Ni57Ti18.1Zr19.4Si5.5) and 19 (Ni58Ti16.9Zr19.1Si6) show a composite structure of primary phase (dark region) plus amorphous matrix (bright featureless region). Micrographs of alloys 11 (Ni57.5Ti18.1Zr19.4Si5) and 20 (Ni58.5Ti17.2Zr19.3Si5) are featureless, indicating a fully amorphous microstructure. However, for alloys 1 and 13, as shown in Fig. 7 a and b, some primary dendrites occur in the amorphous matrix. The corresponding XRD patterns for alloys 1, 13 and 19 confirm the formation of a composite structure with amorphous phase plus crystalline phases (as shown in Fig. 8 ). Such crystalline phases are composed of mainly Ni10(Zr,Ti)7 and a few Ni(Ti,Zr) phases, which is consistent with the earlier reported results [16,17]. For comparison, the XRD patterns of alloys 11, 12 and 20 all present a characteristic of amorphous structure with broad humps. Despite no crystalline peak is detected in the XRD patterns, however, there exhibits a small amount of crystalline phases for alloy 12, as shown in Fig. 6a.

Fig. 9 shows the XRD patterns and DSC curves of the as-cast rod and as-spun ribbon of alloy 11. The XRD pattern of the as-cast rod consists of only a broad hump, and no diffraction peak associated with a crystalline phase is seen, indicating a single glassy phase. The values of the T g, T x and ΔH for the as-cast rod sample are 809K, 868K and 57J/g, respectively. The corresponding values for the ribbons are 811K, 870K and 53J/g, respectively. No marked differences between these two samples can be found, further confirming that the 2mm cast rod of alloy 11 is fully amorphous.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Composition range for bulk glass formation
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 illustrates bulk glass alloys with 2mm diameter are fabricated in a narrow composition range of 57.5–58.5at.% Ni, 36.5–38.5at.% (Ti+Zr) and 5–5.5at.% Si, in which a strong GFA dependency on composition can be found. The glass-forming zone is only 0.5–1.5at.% away from the best alloy of Ni57Ti18Zr20Si5 [9] reported and is enclosed by a region capable of only forming composites. This composite region is in turn bounded by the fully crystalline region. The values of the T g, T x, T m, T L, ΔT x, T rg and ΔH of all glassy alloys are summarized in Table 2 . Our results show clearly that the best glass-forming alloys are at off eutectic composition. This result is consistent with those reported earlier in the La- [18] and Zr- [19] based alloys. The ΔT x and T rg of our bulk glass alloys are in the range of 45–60 and 0.608–0.630K, respectively. In these glassy alloys, no distinct correlation is found between GFA and the values of ΔT x or T rg . Our best glassy alloys are obtained by carefully studying the microstructure changes in these samples. As Si content increases, the microstructure change from fully crystalline phases of mainly Ni10(Zr,Ti)7 (alloy 2, Ni57Ti18.8Zr21.2Si3), to primary Ni10(Zr,Ti)7 in a glass matrix (alloy 1, Ni57Ti18Zr20Si5), then to fully amorphous (alloy 14), finally to crystalline with a Si-containing phase (alloy 3, Ni57Ti16.9Zr19.1Si7). The best glass-forming alloys in our study are enclosed by the alloys which show the changes in the microstructure from Ni10(Zr,Ti)7 for alloy 2 (Fig. 4a) to a Si-containing phase in alloy 3 (Fig. 4b). This result gives us important hints that it is essential to carefully monitor the microstructure change in the cross section of the cast rods in search of the best glass-forming alloys in the BMG systems.

All the Ni–(Ti,Zr)–Si alloys studied have their solidus temperature around 1257K, indicating they correspond to the same one eutectic reaction. The 2mm amorphous alloys are fabricated in a narrow composition region and the GFA is strong composition-dependence in this system. The fully amorphous zone is found to be surrounded by a partial amorphous-forming (composite-forming) region, while the composite-forming region enclosed by the crystalline region with distinctly different structures. The main competing crystalline phases, identified by XRD and SEM, are Ni10(Zr,Ti)7, Ni(Ti,Zr) and an unidentified Si-containing phase. This work gives us a useful way to locate the best glass former by means of monitoring the microstructure evolution around the glass-forming region.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 50271070 and 50323009). The authors are also with the Multi-Component Amorphous and Nanocrystalline Systems (MANS) Research Team, funded by the Chinese Academy of Sciences. We also would like to thank Prof J. Xu for valuable discussion.
